title: Sealed-boundary guard ignores the Critic's `critique` field — chrome can enter scored context undetected by BOTH the role guard and the kernel re-check
worst_realistic_consequence: When the Critic is enabled (enable_critic=True), the critique the model produces is appended to the scored context as a message whose text lives in the `critique` field, not `content`. assert_no_chrome_leak — the load-bearing andon, run by both roles._ChromeGuard and the kernel's post-Critic _assert_no_chrome_leak_if_present — only inspects message['content']. A Tier-3 chrome value (a bare rank/cohort_size number, or a solver-id) that appears in the critique text therefore passes the fail-closed boundary check silently. Motivation (chrome) and measurement then share a context window for that attempt and every downstream turn that re-reads messages, with NO andon halt. The whole purpose of the guard — fail toward 'possible leak' over 'missed leak' — is defeated for an entire class of scored-context message. The instrument can grade a contaminated attempt as clean.
description: engagement.assert_no_chrome_leak walks `messages` and reads only `message.get('content', '')`. The Critic role (roles.py:288) appends `self.message(critique)` to `state.messages`, and Critic.message (roles.py:270-274) returns `{'role': 'critic', 'critique': critique, 'anonymized': anonymized}` — the model-produced text is under `critique`, and there is no `content` key, so `.get('content','')` returns `''` and the critique is never scanned. The kernel's post-Critic re-check (kernel.py:398 -> _assert_no_chrome_leak_if_present -> assert_no_chrome_leak) iterates the same messages and inherits the same blind spot. I confirmed this empirically: a Chrome with leaderboard=[{'solver':'solver-zeta'}] and a critique 'The leading entry is from solver-zeta; surpass it.' raises nothing at either the engagement guard, the role _ChromeGuard, or the kernel re-check. Note the existing test test_kernel.test_chrome_leak_after_critic_turn_raises gives FALSE assurance: it injects the leak via an in-place edit of messages[0]['content'] (which IS scanned), not via the critique field the Critic actually populates — so it does not exercise the real Critic message shape and cannot detect this hole. Plausibility caveat (why HIGH not CRITICAL): the model never sees chrome, so a string-rendered chrome token reproduced verbatim in a critique is unlikely; a bare numeric rank/cohort_size colliding with a number the critique mentions is more plausible. Still, the guard's documented contract is to catch a leak 'however the value was interpolated into a prompt' across scored context, and an entire scored-context field is unscanned.
proposed_fix_sketch: Make the guard scan ALL string-valued fields of each message (or at minimum a known set: content + critique), not just `content`. E.g. iterate message.values() and stringify/scan every str (mirroring _stringify's value-walk), or have the guard accept a message-text extractor. Separately, fix test_chrome_leak_after_critic_turn_raises to leak via the Critic's actual appended critique field (chrome value placed in the critique text) so the test proves the post-Critic re-check covers the Critic's real message shape, and add an engagement-level unit test asserting a chrome token in a `critique`-field message raises.

title: Post-Critic re-check test asserts coverage it does not exercise (in-place content edit instead of the Critic's appended critique message)
worst_realistic_consequence: The test suite's strongest claim about the Critic message-mutating site is unfounded. test_chrome_leak_after_critic_turn_raises is relied on (per the module docstring and the H1 §10.1(e) rationale) to prove the kernel re-runs the sealed-boundary andon over the Critic turn. But it only proves the re-check catches an in-place edit of an EXISTING content-bearing message — a path build_scored_context already guarantees has a `content` key. It never drives a leak through the field the Critic itself writes (`critique`). So the gate the team believes protects the Critic turn is green while the real Critic leak vector (kernel-core-001) sails through. A reviewer reading this passing test would conclude the Critic boundary is sealed when it is not.
description: _critic_leaking_generate (test_kernel.py:520) leaks by mutating attempt.messages[0]['content'] in place on the Critic's generate call. The Critic role does NOT route its output through that edit; it APPENDS a new message dict with the text under `critique` (roles.py:288/270). Thus the test exercises a content-field path that is already covered, and leaves the actual Critic-appended-message path (critique field) untested. Combined with kernel-core-001, this is a test that proves less than the docstring claims it proves about the post-Critic andon.
evidence:
async def _gen(attempt: AttemptState) -> str:
        state["calls"] += 1
        if state["calls"] == 1:
            return _CORRECT_ANSWER  # Solver: clean
        # Critic turn: in-place leak the kernel's post-Critic re-check must catch.
        if attempt.messages:
            attempt.messages[0] = dict(attempt.messages[0])
            attempt.messages[0]["content"] = (
                str(attempt.messages[0].get("content", "")) + f" [leak: {token}]"
            )
        return "a critique"
proposed_fix_sketch: Add (or convert) a test where the enabled Critic's generate RETURNS a critique string containing a chrome token, so the leak rides in the Critic's own appended `{role:'critic','critique':...}` message; assert the kernel post-Critic re-check raises SealedBoundaryViolation. This test will fail until kernel-core-001 is fixed, correctly turning the gate RED on the real vector.

title: Observability counts a PASSED oracle gate as UNSOLVED when net score is non-positive (pass^k / solve-rate / graduation miscount)
worst_realistic_consequence: A Solver that cleanly passes the §8.3 conjunctive gate (solved, no regression, over threshold, no critical penalty, within budget) but whose tiebreaker net score lands <= 0 because a triggered REGRESSIONAL/minor penalty outweighs solve+bonuses is silently recorded as UNSOLVED in every observability rollup. pass^k, solve_rate, novelty rollups, and the §1 Wilson-based graduation rule (is_graduation_candidate / roll_up) are all computed off these mis-derived outcomes, so the instrument under-reports solves and can flip a puzzle's graduation verdict or a model's leaderboard standing. The grading authority (gate_passed) and the reported reliability metric disagree — the instrument silently lies about a measurement, which is the explicit CRITICAL/HIGH bar for this repo.
description: The oracle's grade() returns Score.value = net (solve + elegance + novelty + penalties, penalties negative) on a PASSED gate, else 0.0 (oracle.py:211-212). The kernel stores this exact Score under attempt.scores['oracle'] (kernel.py:378) with no remap. observability._attempt_solved() then re-derives 'solved' from _score_is_truthy(score.value), which for a numeric value returns value > 0. Penalties are NOT bounded relative to solve (only elegance <=30% and novelty <=50% of solve are bounded upstream), so a passing-gate attempt can have net <= 0 (e.g. solve=10, regressional penalty=-50 -> net=-40, gate_passed=True). Confirmed live: with such an outcome, grade() reports gate_passed=True value=-40.0 yet _attempt_solved() returns False. A net of exactly 0.0 on a pass (solve cancels penalty) is also miscounted as unsolved. The authoritative gate_passed flag sits in score.metadata and is ignored by the solve-detection path.
proposed_fix_sketch: Solve-detection must key on the authoritative gate verdict, not the tiebreaker net. In _attempt_solved, prefer score.metadata['gate_passed'] when present: `gp = score.metadata.get('gate_passed'); if isinstance(gp, bool): return gp`. Only fall back to _score_is_truthy(value) for scores that lack the oracle gate envelope (e.g. a bool 'C'/True Inspect-style score). Add a regression test: a passing gate whose net is negative/zero must roll up as n_solved==1.

title: EXTERNAL_VERIFIER family exclusion is case-sensitive exact-string match with no normalization — a same-family judge survives on a tag-casing/synonym mismatch
worst_realistic_consequence: If the kernel tags the model-under-measurement's generator_family as 'claude' but a judge seat is tagged 'Claude', 'claude-opus', or 'anthropic' (a casing or synonym drift in the family vocabulary), eligible_judges() does NOT drop that judge, so a model from the generator's own family votes on its own family's output — the exact self-preference bias EXTERNAL_VERIFIER (workflow-standard 6, §10.2) exists to structurally prevent. The panel's verdict (including the novelty_validated the kernel feeds into the oracle gate) is then contaminated by an in-distribution judge. Exploitability depends on the kernel's tag discipline, so this is a brittleness/defense-in-depth gap rather than a guaranteed breach — hence MEDIUM, not HIGH.
description: Exclusion is `judge_family(j) != self.generator_family`, a raw Python string inequality. There is no case-folding, trimming, or canonicalization of the family label on either side, and untagged judges (family None) are intentionally never excluded. Confirmed live: generator_family='claude' with a judge tagged 'Claude' yields excluded=[] and eligible_count=2 — the capitalized same-family judge stays in the panel. The module docstring frames exclusion as 'structural', but the structure is only as strong as exact-string agreement between two independently-set tags (the kernel's generator tag and the seat.family from JudgmentRecord). A controlled vocabulary is assumed but not enforced.
proposed_fix_sketch: Normalize both sides before comparison (casefold + strip), e.g. `def _norm(f): return f.casefold().strip() if f else f` and compare `_norm(judge_family(j)) != _norm(self.generator_family)`. Stronger: validate family labels against an enum/closed vocabulary at tag time so 'claude' vs 'anthropic' vs 'claude-opus' cannot diverge, and add a test asserting a case/whitespace-variant same-family judge is excluded.

title: Oracle grade() never surfaces a `novelty_validated` key, so observability novelty-rate silently reads 0 off the oracle score
worst_realistic_consequence: ModelProfile.novelty_rate (the §8.7 leaderboard column ai_crucible says it 'most wants to measure') can under-report a model's validated-novelty rate to 0 when an attempt carries only an 'oracle' score and no 'panel' score. _is_novel looks for metadata['novelty_validated'] on the oracle (or panel) score, but grade() records novelty only as components['novelty'] (a numeric bonus) and never writes a novelty_validated key. The leaderboard then misrepresents the capability the instrument is designed to highlight. Bounded to LOW because the JudgePanel DOES surface novelty_validated and the kernel attaches a 'panel' score whenever a panel runs (kernel.py:386), so the panel-key fallback rescues the normal path; the miscount only bites attempts scored without a panel.
description: observability._is_novel iterates (oracle, panel) score keys and returns True iff bool(score.metadata.get('novelty_validated')). Confirmed live: grade() metadata keys are {attempt_id, components, failed_conditions, gate_passed, has_critical_penalty, puzzle_id, triggered_penalties} — there is no novelty_validated key even when novelty_claimed and novelty_validated were both True and a 40.0 novelty bonus was applied. The novelty signal lives in components['novelty'] > 0, which _is_novel does not inspect. Whether novelty-rate is correct therefore hinges entirely on a 'panel' score being present and carrying the key.
proposed_fix_sketch: Either (a) have grade() stamp metadata['novelty_validated'] = bool(outcome.novelty_claimed and outcome.novelty_validated) so the oracle score self-describes its novelty verdict, or (b) make _is_novel fall back to score.metadata.get('components', {}).get('novelty', 0) > 0 for the oracle key. Option (a) is cleaner and matches the panel's contract.

title: pass^k k<=0 contract diverges between stats.pass_hat_k (raises) and observability.aggregate_pass_hat_k (returns 1.0)
worst_realistic_consequence: The same named reliability metric (pass^k) has two incompatible boundary contracts in two modules. stats.pass_hat_k raises ValueError on k<1; observability.aggregate_pass_hat_k returns 1.0 for k<=0 (and 0.0 for empty outcomes). A caller that swaps one for the other (the docstrings explicitly anticipate Wave-2 sharing one implementation via the injected `interval` hook) gets either a silent 'perfect consistency' (1.0) where the other would have rejected the call, or an exception where the other returns a number. No verdict flips today because k>=1 is always used in roll_up (pass_hat_3); this is a latent inconsistency, hence LOW.
description: stats.pass_hat_k validates `if k < 1: raise ValueError`. observability.aggregate_pass_hat_k instead treats k<=0 as the empty conjunction -> 1.0 and empty outcomes -> 0.0. Both are individually defensible, but they are not the same function for the same metric, and the modules are explicitly intended to converge (observability docstring: 'the kernel (Wave 2) may later inject the scoring module's richer interval'). A future unification that picks the stats contract would turn currently-silent k<=0 callers into exceptions, and vice-versa.
evidence:
if k <= 0:
        return 1.0
    n = len(outcomes)
    if n == 0:
        return 0.0
    successes = sum(1 for o in outcomes if o)
    p = successes / n
    return p**k
proposed_fix_sketch: Pick one boundary contract for pass^k and document it once. If k>=1 is the only valid horizon (consistency over >=1 trial), make both raise on k<1; if the empty-conjunction k=0->1.0 convention is wanted, apply it in both. Add a shared test asserting identical behavior at k in {0, 1} and empty/perfect/never outcome vectors.

title: Bias panel (position/verbosity/family-pref) is structurally inert in the default run yet reports as a PASSING measured gate, not "not measured"
worst_realistic_consequence: In every default characterization run (no human labels), collect_records calls judge_item with position=None and never stamps answer_len/judged_family/judge_family, so position_bias, verbosity_bias, and family_pref_delta all return 0.0 ("not measured"), max_bias is always 0.0, and the quality_score bias penalty never fires. But the profile notes and JudgeProfile fields print position 0.000 / verbosity 0.000 / family-pref +0.000 -> max 0.000 (< 0.25 ceiling) — which reads as a measured PASS. A reviewer (or a downstream report consumer) sees "this judge has no position/verbosity/self-preference bias" when the truth is the instrument never staged a single bias trial. Unlike consistency and ECE — which explicitly emit "not measured" branches — the bias panel claims a clean measurement it never took, so a kin-preferring or position-flipping judge can seat with the bias gate silently rubber-stamping it. This is an instrument-honesty gap on the §11.1 #6 bias defense, not a math error.
description: profile.py lines 490-508 give consistency and ECE explicit "not measured" branches, but the bias panel (510-518) has no such branch: it always formats the three bias numbers as a measured gate with a pass/fail glyph. In the real run path (run.py collect_records -> ollama_adapter.judge_item) none of the bias-trial metadata is ever set, so the bias metrics are definitionally 0.0 and the gate is inert. The metric functions themselves correctly return 0.0 with a "not measured" intent (metrics.py docstrings say so), but the profile layer does not distinguish "measured 0.0 bias" from "no bias trial staged". The asymmetry with the consistency/ECE handling is the bug. Fix is honesty-only (do not fabricate bias trials): detect absence of any position/answer_len/judged_family signal and emit "bias not measured (no swap/length/family trials staged)" exactly as consistency/ECE do, and surface it in the run report.
evidence:
# --- §11.1 #6 bias panel (position / verbosity / family-pref) ---
    pos_b = M.position_bias(records)
    verb_b = M.verbosity_bias(records)
    fam_d = M.family_pref_delta(records)
    bias_ok = m.max_bias < g.bias_ceiling
    notes.append(
        f"bias: position {pos_b:.3f}, verbosity {verb_b:.3f}, family-pref Δ {fam_d:+.3f}"
        f" → max {m.max_bias:.3f} ({'<' if bias_ok else '≥'} {g.bias_ceiling:.2f} ceiling)"
    )
proposed_fix_sketch: Mirror the consistency/ECE "not measured" pattern: compute bias_measured = any(r.position is not None for r in records) or any("answer_len" in r.metadata ...) or any("judged_family" in r.metadata ...). When False, append "bias not measured (no position-swap / verbosity / family trials staged) — no bias penalty" instead of the passing-gate note, and set the corresponding JudgeProfile fields to None so a report consumer cannot read 0.0 as "unbiased". Do NOT synthesize bias trials. Optionally have run.py emit a top-level note that the default run stages no bias trials.

title: Default (model-jury) profile path applies the κ one-sided floor + κ-z hard gate against a hardcoded human_human_kappa=0.80 baseline with no machine-readable provisional caveat
worst_realistic_consequence: In the default run (no --human-labels), build_profile is invoked without human_human_kappa, so it defaults to 0.80 (profile.py build_profile signature). Two HARD gates depend on that number: the one-sided κ floor (κ ≥ 0.80 − margin, a REJECT gate) and the κ-z review flag. There are no humans in this path — 0.80 is a fabricated baseline. The emitted report's alt_test_caveat documents that ω is a circular model-jury bootstrap, but it does NOT carry an equivalent machine-readable caveat that the κ baseline (which can REJECT a judge or seat it) is itself fabricated. A consumer parsing the JSON report could treat a κ-floor REJECT or a human-like SEAT as human-grounded when it is not. Worst realistic outcome: a judge is seated/rejected on a κ comparison to a number no human produced, presented in the report without the provisional flag the omega path gets. The docs (research-grounding.md L743/L788, calibration.md L30) disclose this clearly and seats carry [review] flags, so the human-facing honesty is largely intact — hence LOW.
description: run.py passes human_human_kappa only on the human-grounded branch (line 244, =human_labels.iaa_alpha). The else branch (251-258) omits it, so build_profile's default 0.80 is used for the κ floor and κ-z. The report adds alt_test_caveat for omega circularity but no parallel kappa_baseline_provisional field, even though the κ gate is also un-grounded in this path. metadata["human_human_kappa"] is stamped on each profile (profile.py ~558) so the value is at least recoverable, but it is not labeled provisional/fabricated. This is a disclosure-completeness gap on a hard gate, well-mitigated by the prose docs and review flags.
evidence:
else:
            profile = build_profile(
                model_id,
                RoleSlot.JUDGE,
                recs,
                records_per_annotator=_jury(all_records, model_id, recs),
                quant=quant_by_id.get(model_id),
            )
proposed_fix_sketch: In the model-jury branch, either pass an explicit human_human_kappa with a stamped metadata flag like metadata["kappa_baseline_provisional"]=True, or extend the report with a top-level "kappa_baseline": {"value": 0.80, "source": "hardcoded default — NOT human-estimated; κ floor/z gates are PROVISIONAL until --human-labels supplies iaa_alpha"} alongside alt_test_caveat. Honesty-only; no math change.

title: bump_on_change is a non-enforcing suggester: a changed rubric can keep its old version with zero code-level resistance (the seal that does not seal)
worst_realistic_consequence: A maintainer edits a weight/threshold/judge_prompt (which DOES change the content hash) but leaves new.version="v1.0" unchanged. compile_bundle still produces the correct, distinct hash, so the leaderboard record (model_id, score, bundle_hash) would in principle catch it IF the hash is recorded. But the function the docstring sells as the enforcement primitive — bump_on_change — is (a) never called anywhere in src/ (grep: only the tests reference it), (b) returns a *suggested* string without mutating new.version, and (c) returns old.version on a no-op even when new carried a DIFFERENT label, silently discarding the caller's label. So the §9.1 'version changes iff hash changes' invariant is NOT enforced in code; it rests entirely on the human remembering to assign the return value. The real anti-retconning seal is the recorded bundle_hash, not this function. The hash itself is sound (covers weights+thresholds+judge_prompts, excludes version by design — verified); the gap is that the advertised enforcement does not exist. Severity MEDIUM not HIGH: the hash genuinely fingerprints all scoring content, so a downstream consumer that records the hash WILL detect the change; the defect is an overstated 'enforces' claim plus a missing wire-up, not a hash that fails to cover content.
description: The module docstring (line 22) and the §9.1 grounding state bump_on_change 'enforces the §9.1 invariant: the version changes iff the content hash changes.' In fact it is a pure suggester: it returns a recommended version string and explicitly 'does not mutate either argument'. Nothing in src/ calls it (verified by grep — only test_instrument.py invokes it), and there is no wrapper that asserts new.version == bump_on_change(old, new) before a bundle is published or recorded. Additionally, on a no-op edit it returns old.version even if the caller set a new label, so a well-intentioned relabel of identical content is silently reverted. The genuine protection is compile_bundle's content hash (which I verified covers all four scoring surfaces and correctly excludes the version label); the 'enforces' wording oversells a function that only advises.
proposed_fix_sketch: Either (a) soften the docstring/__init__ claim from 'enforces' to 'advises/computes' and document that the actual seal is the recorded bundle_hash; or (b) add a real gate, e.g. assert_versioned(old, new) that raises RubricBundleError if compile_bundle(old)[0] != compile_bundle(new)[0] and new.version == old.version, and wire it into whatever path records (model_id, score, bundle_hash). Also fix the no-op branch to preserve new.version when content is identical but new explicitly relabeled.

title: _next_version mangles date-style version labels (2026.06 -> 2026.7), producing a misleading version string on a real rubric change
worst_realistic_consequence: If a maintainer uses a calendar-style rubric version like 'v2026.06' or '2026.06' (common for date-pinned eval artifacts) and changes the scoring content, bump_on_change returns '2026.7' / 'v2026.7' — dropping the zero-pad and incrementing the month segment to a value that reads as July. The version still provably changes (so the weak invariant 'label differs' holds), but the resulting label is semantically wrong and could be mistaken for a different (later-month) release in release docs, undermining the human-readable audit trail the version field exists to provide. Low severity because the content hash remains the authoritative identity and the label change is still detectable.
description: _next_version treats the trailing [0-9.] run as a dotted numeric and increments the last numeric segment via int() -> str(), which strips leading zeros. Verified empirically: '2026.06' -> '2026.7', 'v1.' -> 'v2.' (note the dangling dot survives oddly). The function's contract is only that the label provably changes, which it satisfies, but the produced label is misleading for zero-padded / date-style schemes that are plausible for a measurement instrument pinned to calendar releases.
proposed_fix_sketch: Preserve zero-padding width when incrementing (e.g. zfill to the original segment length), or document that the scheme is intended only for plain semver-style labels and route date-style labels through the '+1' suffix path. At minimum, normalize a trailing dangling '.' so 'v1.' does not yield 'v2.'.

title: _grade_matrix majority-vote collapse feeding prune_items uses >=0.5, breaking ties toward 'correct' and can shift which calibration items survive the IRT screen
worst_realistic_consequence: For an even number of k re-runs with an exact tie (e.g. k=2, one pass one fail), (sum/len)>=0.5 == 0.5>=0.5 collapses to correct=True. This tie-break toward 'pass' feeds the boolean matrix that prune_items screens; a saturated/near-saturated boundary item could be classified as all-pass (zero variance -> dropped) or pushed across the point-biserial floor, changing the kept/dropped calibration set. The instrument's item bank is the foundation of every later measurement, so a systematic optimistic tie-break is a subtle integrity smell. Low severity: it only affects exact ties on even k, the calibration screen is advisory (kept/dropped is reported, not a hard verdict flip), and this file is the consumer (outside the calibration/instrument domain proper) — flagged because it is the real bridge into the in-scope prune_items.
description: This line is in characterize/run.py (the consumer of calibration.irt.prune_items), not in the calibration/ or instrument/ packages I own, so I report it as an adjacent observation rather than an in-domain defect. The majority collapse rounds ties up to True. A neutral instrument would either break ties toward non-solve (conservative) or require odd k. The downstream prune_items math itself (point_biserial corrected item-total, population variance, all-same -> 0.0 -> dropped) I verified to be correct.
evidence: matrix[model_id] = {iid: (sum(v) / len(v)) >= 0.5 for iid, v in by_item.items()}
proposed_fix_sketch: Decide tie semantics explicitly: use > 0.5 (conservative toward non-solve) and document it, or require/assert odd k per item so no tie is possible. Add a test with k=2 split outcomes asserting the chosen tie direction.

title: CLI --models without @family stamps the colliding literal "unknown", silently weakening EXTERNAL_VERIFIER same-family exclusion
worst_realistic_consequence: An operator runs `ai-crucible characterize --models qwenA:32b qwenB:32b` (two checkpoints of the SAME real family, or two different families) without `@family`. Both are tagged family="unknown". The family exclusion (JudgePanel.eligible_judges) keys on exact string equality, so a generator also tagged "unknown" does NOT exclude a same-distribution "unknown" judge: a model rates its own family's output and no error is raised. EXTERNAL_VERIFIER — the single strongest self-preference mitigation (research-grounding §3/§10.2) — is silently broken, and the instrument reports a 'cross-family' verdict that is actually self-judged.
description: judge_family() documents the conservative contract: an UNTAGGED judge returns None and is treated as external/never-excluded ('we only drop a judge we can prove shares the generator's family'). _parse_models() violates that contract: instead of leaving family unset (None → never excluded, fail-open-but-honest) or refusing the spec, it assigns the real string literal "unknown". "unknown" is a genuine family value that COLLIDES with every other "unknown" and is matched by the exclusion comparison `judge_family(j) != generator_family`. So two genuinely-distinct families both default to "unknown" and are treated as one family (over-exclusion, fail-safe), but worse, a same-real-family judge whose family the operator forgot to tag can survive exclusion against a generator that is also "unknown". research-grounding §11.5/§13 is explicit that family must be the true distributional family ('exact version string ... not family alias', 'never seat two checkpoints of one base family'). The DEFAULT_PANEL is correctly tagged, so the default path is safe; the breach is reachable only via the `--models` operator path, which is why this is HIGH not CRITICAL — but the consequence class (silent same-family judge) is exactly the instrument-lies failure mode.
proposed_fix_sketch: Do not invent a colliding family. Either (a) hard-fail: require `@family` on every --models spec and exit nonzero with a structured error naming the offending spec; or (b) if a permissive mode is wanted, leave family as None (so judge_family→None→treated as external and never silently excluded against another None) — never the shared literal "unknown". Add a test that two specs without @family do NOT compare equal under the exclusion predicate, and a test that a same-family judge is excluded even when families were supplied via the CLI path.

title: OllamaModel never verifies the served model equals the requested model; JudgmentRecord provenance can silently record the wrong model/family
worst_realistic_consequence: Under the documented load->judge->evict / OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=1 serving topology, if a model load times out, an Ollama alias resolves to a different tag, or a previously-loaded model answers, Ollama returns HTTP 200 with a different `model` field in the body. The adapter reads only `r.json()` and discards the served `model`. Every JudgmentRecord is stamped with self.model_id / self.family (the REQUESTED ids) regardless of what actually answered. A model can then be SEATED or REJECTED on the admission test, or excluded by family, based on judgments a *different* model produced — the characterization measurement and the EXTERNAL_VERIFIER family tag are both corrupted with no error and PIN_PER_STEP provenance that lies. The domain prompt flagged this exact 'silent tier-timeout fallback to a different served model' risk.
description: Ollama's /api/chat response echoes the served model in a top-level `model` field. Both _via_httpx and _via_sdk (and _complete_raw) ignore it. _extract_text reads only message.content; judge_item builds the JudgmentRecord with model_id=self.model_id and family=self.family unconditionally, and pin_metadata records the requested model. There is no served==requested check anywhere, so a served!=requested mismatch is undetectable downstream. Because the instrument's whole purpose is reproducible measurement, mis-attributed judgments are a measurement-integrity defect, not a mere observability gap. Rated MEDIUM because it requires an actual server-side mismatch to manifest (no evidence it happens on every run), but the failure is silent and the consequence reaches seat/reject and family-exclusion decisions.
evidence:
async def _via_httpx(**kwargs: Any) -> dict[str, Any]:
            payload = {**kwargs, "stream": False}
            async with httpx.AsyncClient(base_url=host, timeout=600.0) as http:
                r = await http.post("/api/chat", json=payload)
                r.raise_for_status()
                return r.json()   # served `model` field never read/compared
proposed_fix_sketch: In _complete_raw (or judge_item), read the response `model` field and assert/normalize it against self.model_id; on mismatch, either raise (so the kernel's andon halts — matches the adapter's stated 'transport error surfaces as an exception' contract) or stamp the SERVED model_id into the JudgmentRecord and add a `served_mismatch` flag to metadata so the provenance never lies. Add a fake-client test returning a different `model` field and assert the mismatch is surfaced.

title: `ai-crucible characterize` exits 0 even when every model fails and zero judgments are collected
worst_realistic_consequence: Ollama is down / the host is wrong / every model tag is unservable: run_panel catches each model's exception, prints it, and continues; all_records ends empty, profiles ends empty. main() still writes a report (with empty profiles) and returns 0. An automation/CI gate or the dogfood-swarm harness that shells out to `ai-crucible characterize` and checks the exit code treats a run that measured NOTHING as success — silent data loss presented as a green pass, and a stale/empty characterization report can be consumed as if valid.
description: The per-model try/except (`# one bad model must not sink the panel`) is reasonable for ONE bad model, but there is no aggregate check: main() has a single `return 0` and no nonzero path when profiles/all_records is empty. For a measurement instrument, 'collected zero judgments' is a failure, not a success. The CLI dispatcher (cli.py) faithfully forwards this exit code, so the green-on-nothing result propagates to the console-script / npm-launcher entry point that automation calls. This is in characterize/run.py (the target of the `characterize` dispatch), squarely the 'exit codes nonzero on failure?' concern called out for the models-cli/CLI domain.
evidence:
args.out.write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2, default=str), encoding="utf-8")
    seated = [m for m, p in profiles.items() if p.seat_decision.value == "seat"]
    print(f"\nseated: {seated}")
    ...
    print(f"report -> {args.out}")
    return 0   # <- always 0, even when profiles=={} / all_records=={}
proposed_fix_sketch: After run_panel, if not profiles (or not all_records) emit a structured error (code/message/hint) to stderr and `return 1` (distinct from the dispatcher's `2` for unknown-command). Optionally a non-fatal warning + nonzero when some-but-not-all models failed. Add a CLI test with a panel whose only model raises, asserting a nonzero exit.

title: CLI usage banner hardcodes "v0.2.0" while --version reads it from package metadata (drift on next bump)
worst_realistic_consequence: On the next version bump, `pyproject.toml`/`__init__.__version__`/the installed metadata move forward but the `_USAGE` string still says 'research preview, v0.2.0'. `ai-crucible --help` then reports a stale version that disagrees with `ai-crucible --version`. Cosmetic/honesty drift in the front-door banner; no measurement impact. Currently consistent (all 0.2.0), so this is a latent maintenance trap, not a present bug.
description: _version() correctly resolves from importlib.metadata with a source-tree fallback, but the help banner embeds the literal 'v0.2.0' instead of interpolating _version(). Two sources of truth for the same fact in the same file.
proposed_fix_sketch: Interpolate _version() into the banner (e.g. build _USAGE in a function or use an f-string at print time) so --help and --version cannot disagree.

title: Coverage badge claims 96% but actual is 91.5%, and the CI gate floor is only 60%
worst_realistic_consequence: A reader (or the dogfood-swarm shipcheck scorecard, which is required to reflect ACTUAL gate results not estimates) trusts a 96% coverage badge that overstates the real 91.5% figure by ~4.5 points. Because the only enforced gate is pyproject `fail_under = 60`, nothing in CI ties the badge to reality, so the number can drift arbitrarily far from the truth without any test going red. This is a self-measuring research instrument whose entire credibility rests on honest provenance; an inflated badge on the front door is exactly the kind of unbacked claim the shipcheck honesty gate exists to catch.
description: The README coverage badge hard-codes `coverage-96%` while the audited actual coverage is 91.5%. The CI workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml lines 61-72) derives its `--cov-fail-under` from `pyproject.toml [tool.coverage.report].fail_under`, which is 60 — so CI passes at any coverage >=60% and never validates the 96% badge claim. The badge is a static shields.io URL, not generated from a coverage run, so it is a hand-maintained number that has drifted above the real value. Note this is a documentation/honesty issue, not a broken test; the test SUITE itself is sound. Per the project's shipcheck rule 'Scorecard reflects ACTUAL gate results from shipcheck audit, not estimates', the badge should match the measured number.
evidence: README.md:13: pyproject.toml:78: fail_under = 60 .github/workflows/ci.yml (covfloor step): floor=$(uv run python -c "... ['tool']['coverage']['report']['fail_under']") --cov-fail-under=${{ steps.covfloor.outputs.floor }}
proposed_fix_sketch: Either (a) regenerate the badge from the real measured coverage (91% / 92%) so it matches, or (b) wire a coverage-badge step that writes the actual number after the CI cov run, and raise pyproject `fail_under` to a floor that actually protects the claim (e.g. 90) so the badge cannot silently drift above the gate again.

title: The real-run driver in characterize/run.py (collect_records / run_panel / main) is untested, including a measurement-biasing parse-fail branch
worst_realistic_consequence: The judge-admission characterization run — the pipeline that turns model outputs into the SEAT/SCREEN/REJECT decisions and the composed cross-family panel the instrument scores with — has no test on its grading loop. In `collect_records`, an unparseable judge verdict (`parsed is None`) is silently scored as the WRONG answer (`pred_num = 1 - gold_num`) rather than recorded as an abstention. A judge whose output format the regex misses thus has its measured objective_accuracy / Cohen's kappa / alt-test omega biased downward, which can flip its seat decision (REJECT a judge that is actually fine, or distort the reliability weights that drive the weighted panel verdict). Because run.py sits at 44% coverage and `collect_records`/`run_panel`/`main` have zero tests, this measurement-distorting behavior could change or break and the suite (relied on as the 473-test gate) would stay green — a gate that proves nothing about the path that produces the actual measurements.
description: tests/test_characterize_run.py only exercises the PURE helpers (_parse_models, parse_choice, _to_num, _jury, known_groups_report, panel_correlation_report, irt_prune_report, panel_composition_report). The measurement-bearing functions collect_records (the per-item k-rerun grading loop), run_panel (the two-pass load->judge->evict->profile orchestrator with the human-labels vs circular model-jury branch at lines 232-258), perturbation_report, calibration_report, and main (the report assembler + --write-panel/--human-labels integration) have NO direct test. The most consequential untested line is the parse-failure-to-wrong-answer collapse at run.py:144 (`pred_num = _to_num(parsed) if parsed is not None else (1 - gold_num)`). Treating a malformed/unparseable verdict as a confident wrong answer (rather than dropping it or flagging it) is a measurement-integrity decision that should have a test pinning the intended behavior; right now nothing characterizes it. This is the coverage blind-spot the audit prompt flagged (characterize/run.py 44%), and it matters because these are the lines that produce the instrument's outputs, not cosmetic glue.
evidence:
src/ai_crucible/characterize/run.py:140-148 (collect_records, untested):
        for ri in range(k):
            rec = await model.judge_item(item.prompt, run_index=ri)
            rec.item_id = item.id
            parsed = parse_choice(str(rec.predicted), gold_str)
            pred_num = _to_num(parsed) if parsed is not None else (1 - gold_num)
            rec.predicted = pred_num
            rec.gold = gold_num
            rec.correct = parsed == gold_str

(grep confirms only `irt_prune_report` and `cli.main` are referenced from tests; collect_records / run_panel / def main / perturbation_report / calibration_report appear in NO test file.)
proposed_fix_sketch: Add tests that drive collect_records with a fake OllamaModel returning (a) a clean parseable verdict, (b) an unparseable verdict — and assert the intended handling explicitly (decide and pin: is an unparseable output a wrong answer or an abstention? right now it is silently scored wrong). Add a thin run_panel/main test using an injected fake panel (monkeypatch OllamaModel) over the sample items to cover the orchestration, the model-jury vs human-labels branch, and the report assembly + --write-panel path. This lifts the 44% blind spot on the path that produces the actual measurements.

title: Symlink-escape security tests skip on Windows without SeCreateSymbolicLinkPrivilege
worst_realistic_consequence: The two sandbox symlink-escape containment tests (read and write) are the proof that a symlink planted inside the workdir cannot be used to read/write outside the §10.4 sealed boundary. On a Windows developer machine without symlink privilege they pytest.skip, so a developer running the suite locally on Windows sees them pass-as-skipped and could believe the symlink boundary is verified when it was never exercised. The behavior is still covered on ubuntu-latest CI (where symlinks always create), so this is a local-visibility gap rather than an unguarded boundary — the escape is also defended-by-construction via Path.resolve() containment. Hence LOW, not HIGH.
description: _make_symlink_or_skip calls pytest.skip when os.symlink raises (Windows without Developer Mode/admin). The skip is honest and documented, and the primary CI runner is ubuntu-latest (per github-actions rule), where the tests run. This is not a masked bug — the containment is structural — but it does mean the symlink-escape security property is unverified on the Windows dev rig this repo is developed on, with no compensating assertion. Worth noting because the prompt explicitly asks about OS-specific skips that could mask real bugs; here the underlying behavior is sound but the local proof is absent.
evidence:
tests/test_sandbox.py:263-268:
    try:
        os.symlink(target, link)
    except (OSError, NotImplementedError) as exc:  # pragma: no cover - env-dependent
        pytest.skip(f"cannot create symlinks on this runner: {exc}")
    if not link.is_symlink():  # pragma: no cover - defensive
        pytest.skip("symlink creation did not produce a symlink on this runner")
proposed_fix_sketch: Either gate CI to guarantee the symlink tests run on at least one runner (already true via ubuntu-latest) and document that in the test, or add a non-symlink unit test of the containment helper (_resolve_within) that exercises the same rejection path on every OS so the security property has an always-running proof independent of symlink-creation privilege.

title: README coverage badge claims 96% but actual coverage is 91.5%
worst_realistic_consequence: An operator/reviewer assessing the instrument's test rigor trusts a stated 96% line coverage when the real number from `pytest --cov` is 91.50%. The badge is a green, hardcoded shields.io image (not coverage-service-driven), so it can never self-correct and overstates rigor by ~4.5 points on the project's most-read honesty surface. Not verdict-flipping math, hence MEDIUM, but it is a confirmed false numeric claim on the front door.
description: The README header badge hardcodes `coverage-96%25` with alt text 'Coverage 96%'. Running the suite the CI/verify path runs (`uv run pytest --cov=ai_crucible`) reports TOTAL 91.50% across 473 passing tests. 96% appears nowhere in the actual measurement; it is the only coverage number on any honesty surface (SCORECARD/SHIP_GATE/CHANGELOG cite the 60% floor, not a headline coverage figure) and it is wrong. Correct value: 91% (91.50%).
evidence: README.md:13 --- actual, from `uv run pytest --cov=ai_crucible` --- TOTAL 3081 262 91% Required test coverage of 60.0% reached. Total coverage: 91.50% ============================ 473 passed in 20.35s =============================
proposed_fix_sketch: Claim is 'coverage 96%'; correct value is 91% (91.50% measured). Update the badge src+alt to `coverage-91%25` / 'Coverage 91%' (coordinator-owned prose change — flag, do not rewrite).

title: SECURITY.md §1 describes the Solver container as a shipped, hardened Docker container; only a local-subprocess sandbox actually ships
worst_realistic_consequence: An operator reading the threat model believes the Solver runs in a hardened, network-isolated, read-only, cap-dropped, digest-pinned Docker container TODAY, and configures/trusts the instrument accordingly — when the only shipped provider is `LocalSandbox`, a local subprocess with path-confinement and no network/seccomp/cap-drop enforcement at all. The doc's own honest-residual-risk note ('a plain Linux container is not a hard boundary') reinforces the false impression that a container exists. This over-trust of an unshipped containment layer is exactly the over-trust SECURITY.md says it exists to prevent. It is not a boundary breach (the real lock is the out-of-band oracle, which IS architecturally sound and shipped), so MEDIUM not HIGH.
description: SECURITY.md §1 states in present tense that the Solver 'runs in a locked container' that 'is hardened: digest-pinned per-puzzle image, cap_drop: ALL, default seccomp, no-new-privileges, non-root, read_only rootfs, network_mode: none', and §No-telemetry repeats 'Solver containers run network_mode: none'. The shipped code (sandbox.py) only provides `LocalSandbox` (subprocess), and its OWN docstring is explicit that 'The hardened Docker / Cloud-Hypervisor microVM provider is a later wave' and that those properties belong to 'a future container's' provider. CHANGELOG Notes confirm 'the hardened container/microVM sandbox provider ... deferred to Phase 2+'. The SECURITY.md present-tense framing contradicts the code and the CHANGELOG. README.md:41 compounds this by describing the sandbox module as a 'locked, network-less container'.
evidence:
SECURITY.md:19-22
The Solver runs in a locked container reached only through a narrow
`exec` / `read_file` / `write_file` channel (no raw shell). The container is
hardened: digest-pinned per-puzzle image, `cap_drop: ALL`, default seccomp,
`no-new-privileges`, non-root, `read_only` rootfs, `network_mode: none`.

--- contradicted by sandbox.py:6 ---
hook. The hardened Docker / Cloud-Hypervisor microVM provider is a later wave; the
--- and CHANGELOG.md:45-47 ---
  cross-family local-model panel (Phase 2), the hardened container/microVM sandbox
  provider, ... are deferred to Phase 2+ and are documented as such.
proposed_fix_sketch: Claim is 'the container is hardened: digest-pinned ... read_only rootfs, network_mode: none' (present tense). Correct value: the shipped provider is a local subprocess (LocalSandbox); the hardened container is Phase-2+. Reword §1 + the no-telemetry bullet + README.md:41 to future/conditional tense ('the Phase-2 container provider will run network_mode: none') — coordinator-owned prose; flag only.

title: SHIP_GATE.md and SCORECARD.md classify the repo as 'not a CLI / not an npm package'; the repo ships both a CLI and an npm package
worst_realistic_consequence: The ship gate's hard-gate-B/C/D evaluation SKIPs every [cli] and [npm] item ('SKIP: library, no CLI entry point', 'SKIP: not an npm package') on the stated basis that the repo has no CLI/npm surface. But pyproject declares `[project.scripts] ai-crucible = ...`, src/ai_crucible/cli.py is a full argparse CLI with --help/--version/exit-codes 0/2, __main__.py backs `python -m ai_crucible`, npm/package.json publishes `@dogfood-lab/ai-crucible` with a bin, and release.yml builds+ships PyInstaller binaries and npm-publishes. So gate items that SHOULD have been actively verified ([cli] exit codes 0/1/2/3, [cli] --help accurate, [npm] npm pack dry-run, [npm] lockfile) were skipped under a false premise — the gate certifies 'done' for surfaces it never checked. A gate that passes by mis-declaring its own subject proves less than it claims. MEDIUM (not HIGH) because the CLI/npm surfaces do appear functional on inspection; the defect is the gate's integrity, not a shipped runtime bug.
description: SHIP_GATE.md line 8 asserts 'This repo is: a Python library/framework. It is not a CLI, MCP server, desktop, or npm package — those tagged items are SKIPped with reasons.' Lines 30, 41, 52 then SKIP the [cli] exit-codes, [cli] --help, and [npm] npm-pack items as N/A. SCORECARD.md line 6 likewise tags it '(Python library/framework — not CLI/MCP/desktop/npm)'. Reality: pyproject.toml:40-41 `[project.scripts] ai-crucible = "ai_crucible.cli:main"`; cli.py implements --help/--version and returns exit code 2 on unknown command; __main__.py is the documented `python -m ai_crucible` / PyInstaller entry; npm/package.json:2 name `@dogfood-lab/ai-crucible` with bin/ai-crucible.js; release.yml smoke-tests `dist/<binary> --version` and runs `npm publish --provenance`. The cli.py header itself calls it 'the ai-crucible CLI — the unified entry point'. The classification used to gate the release is false, and it caused real gate items to be skipped rather than verified.
evidence:
SHIP_GATE.md:8
**This repo is:** a Python **library/framework** (`[all]` + `[pypi]`). It is not a CLI, MCP server, desktop, or npm package — those tagged items are SKIPped with reasons.
SHIP_GATE.md:30
- [ ] `[cli]` Exit codes: 0/1/2/3 — SKIP: library, no CLI entry point.
SHIP_GATE.md:52
- [ ] `[npm]` `npm pack --dry-run` — SKIP: not an npm package.

--- contradicted by pyproject.toml:40-41 ---
[project.scripts]
ai-crucible = "ai_crucible.cli:main"
--- and cli.py:1 ---
"""ai-crucible CLI — the unified entry point.
--- and npm/package.json:2,10-12 ---
  "name": "@dogfood-lab/ai-crucible",
  "bin": { "ai-crucible": "bin/ai-crucible.js" },
proposed_fix_sketch: Claim is 'not a CLI ... or npm package'; correct value is '[all]+[pypi]+[cli]+[npm]'. The [cli] and [npm] gate rows should be actively checked (exit codes via cli.py, --help text, `npm pack --dry-run` on npm/, lockfile), not SKIPped. Coordinator-owned: re-tag the repo type in SHIP_GATE.md:8 + SCORECARD.md:6 and re-evaluate the wrongly-skipped rows.

title: SHIP_GATE.md states the built wheel is 'crucible-0.2.0'; the actual artifact is ai_crucible-0.2.0
worst_realistic_consequence: A reader verifying the packaging line is told `uv build` produces a `crucible-0.2.0` wheel; the real artifacts are `ai_crucible-0.2.0-py3-none-any.whl` / `ai_crucible-0.2.0.tar.gz` (project name is `ai-crucible`, PyPI-normalized to `ai_crucible`). 'crucible' is not the package name anywhere. Cosmetic doc inaccuracy with no runtime impact, hence LOW.
description: SHIP_GATE.md line 54 records the [pypi] clean-build evidence as 'uv build → crucible-0.2.0 wheel + sdist'. Building confirms the artifacts are named `ai_crucible-0.2.0` (matching pyproject name `ai-crucible`). The 'crucible-' stem is a stale/abbreviated name.
evidence:
SHIP_GATE.md:54
- [x] `[npm]` Lockfile committed · `[pypi]` Clean wheel + sdist build (2026-06-01 — `uv build` → `crucible-0.2.0` wheel + sdist; `uv.lock` committed)

--- actual `uv build` output ---
Successfully built dist\ai_crucible-0.2.0.tar.gz
Successfully built dist\ai_crucible-0.2.0-py3-none-any.whl
proposed_fix_sketch: Claim 'crucible-0.2.0 wheel'; correct value 'ai_crucible-0.2.0'. Coordinator-owned single-token fix in the evidence note.
